coredata.dc:title: Copper and nitrogen-doped carbon dots as an anti-interference fluorescent probe combined with magnetic material purification for nicotine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xueqin
Chen, Zhiyan
Li, Qiulan
Meng, Dongling
Jiang, Honglin
Zhou, Yun
Feng, Shouai
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a spectrofluorimetric method combined with magnetic solid-phase extraction purification was utilized for the nicotine detection in shredded tobacco. The copper and nitrogen doped carbon dots (Cu-NCDs) with a high quantum yield (30%) was prepared via facile microwave method and used as fluorescent probe for nicotine assay. Fe3O4 nanoparticles were used to remove the coexisting substances in the tobaccos by physical adsorption. The Cu-NCDs showed two fluorescence emission peaks at 294 nm and at 580 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 270 nm, and could be obviously quenched by nicotine at the same time. Herein, highly sensitive detection of nicotine in shredded tobacco was developed. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-NCDs was linearly proportional to the nicotine concentration in the range of 0.1 to 10 μg mL−1, with the detection limit of 0.01 μg mL−1. The combination of Fe3O4 adsorbent and fluorescent probe showed improved anti-interference performance in comparison to fluorescent probe alone. Based on results from the study, the method could be utilized as a fluorescent probe for the practical determination of nicotine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Magnetic materials
Nicotine
Purification
Tobacco

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Cu, N-doped carbon dots (Cu-NCDs)
Tobacco sample purification by magnetic solid phase extraction
Spectrum properties and stability of Cu-NCDs
Purification and selectivity of the method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for fabrication Cu-NCDs-based nanoprobe for assaying nicotine.
TEM image (a), FTIR spectra (b), XPS spectra (c), fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis absorption spectra (d) of prepared Cu-NCDs.

Fluorescence spectra of Cu-NCDs in the presence of nicotine (0–10 μg mL−1), the inset is the linear relationship between the fluorescence change of Cu-NCDs vs. nicotine concentration at 294 nm and 580 nm.
Purification effect of Fe3O4 on tobacco substrate (a); Effect of metal ions (b), sugars and amino acids (c), organic acids (d).
Analytical results for tobacco samples by the proposed method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: “()” means that the sample is purified by MSPE technology.

head.ce:title: Copper and nitrogen-doped carbon dots as an anti-interference fluorescent probe combined with magnetic material purification for nicotine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Center of China Tobacco Guangxi Industrial Co., Ltd., Guangxi Province 530001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Center of China Tobacco Guangxi Industrial Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technology Center of China Tobacco Guangxi Industrial Co., Ltd., Guangxi Province 530001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a spectrofluorimetric method combined with magnetic solid-phase extraction purification was utilized for the nicotine detection in shredded tobacco. The copper and nitrogen doped carbon dots (Cu-NCDs) with a high quantum yield (30%) was prepared via facile microwave method and used as fluorescent probe for nicotine assay. Fe3O4 nanoparticles were used to remove the coexisting substances in the tobaccos by physical adsorption. The Cu-NCDs showed two fluorescence emission peaks at 294 nm and at 580 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 270 nm, and could be obviously quenched by nicotine at the same time. Herein, highly sensitive detection of nicotine in shredded tobacco was developed. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-NCDs was linearly proportional to the nicotine concentration in the range of 0.1 to 10 μg mL−1, with the detection limit of 0.01 μg mL−1. The combination of Fe3O4 adsorbent and fluorescent probe showed improved anti-interference performance in comparison to fluorescent probe alone. Based on results from the study, the method could be utilized as a fluorescent probe for the practical determination of nicotine.

ce:section.ce:para: Nicotine (1-methyl-2(3-pyridyl)pyrrolidine) is the main alkaloid found in tobacco plants, accounting for about 1.5% of the total content of tobacco, which is considered to be the main cause of cigarette addiction throughout the world [1]. Nicotiana species contains many alkaloids, phenolic compounds and other compounds, and the most abundant groups of which is nicotine. Because the nicotine is both addictive and toxic in tobacco [2], many countries make laws and regulations prohibiting people from smoking in public places [3,4]. The quantitative analysis of nicotine has always been the focus of tobacco quality evaluation. At present, several analytical methods based on different techniques were developed to detect nicotine in tobacco and tobacco products, including electrochemical [4], liquid/gas chromatography [5,6], capillary electrophoresis [7], colorimetric assay [8] and fluorescence sensor [9]. As a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) can be used as a fluorescence sensor for the determination of alkaloids. CDs has significant advantages in terms of high chemical stability [10], excellent water solubility [11], photobleaching resistance, low cost, low toxicity and biocompatibility [12]. CDs can serve as photogenerated electron acceptors or donors, and has photoluminescence properties. To improve the fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) and enlarge the potential application capabilities of CDs, homogeneous transition metal-doped CDs, especially copper-doped CDs, have attracted great interest due to their unique electronic and optical properties [13].

The chemical composition of nicotiana extract is complex, and there are many interfering substances in the matrix, such as alkaloids, flavonoids and terpenes [14]. Currently, the purification of shredded tobacco has received tremendous interest throughout the world. Many separation and purification approaches have been developed and utilized to improve the accuracy and sensitivity of alkaloids detection in tobacco leaves, including liquid–liquid extraction (LLE) [15], liquid–liquid microextraction (LLME) [16,17], solid-phase extraction (SPE) [18], solid-phase microextraction (SPME) [19]. In view of the fact that the combination of magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) and fluorescent probe results in purification performance and detection effect. After using MSPE technique to adsorb impurities in the sample, the fluorescent probe is used to detect the target. Magnetic nanoparticles can be uniformly dispersed in the sample solution by vortex mixing or shaking, resulting in a sufficiently large contact area between the adsorbent and the interference, so that the extraction can be completed in a short time [20,21]. The separation of magnetic nanoparticles can be achieved only by using external magnetism, without centrifugation or filtration [22].

In this work, copper acetate (Cu(CH3COO)2) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was used as the precursor to prepare dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots (Cu-NCDs) that showed two fluorescence emission peaks at 294 nm and at 580 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 270 nm. In order to avoid the interference of coexisting substances in the tobaccos, Fe3O4 NPs as the adsorbent were added to reduce the interference of the matrix in tobacco. Interestingly, an obvious purification effect could be observed, which might be attributed to the adsorption of Fe3O4 NPs on impurities. Although this method requires the preparation of magnetic nanomaterials, it has a strong anti-interference effect compared with other methods. Based on the simultaneous quenching effect of nicotine on the fluorescence peaks at 294 nm and 580 nm, a new, simple and environmentally friendly method was developed to detect nicotine (Fig. 1 ).

Copper acetate (Cu(CH3COO)2) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were purchased from TCI Shanghai Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O), ammonia solution (NH4OH), sodium acetate anhydrous (NaAc), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonium acetate (NH4Ac), ethylene glycol (EG) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Standard of nicotine and tobacco samples were obtained from Technology Center of China Tobacco Guangxi Industrial Co., Ltd. (Nanning, China). Deionized water used in this experiment was prepared with a Milli-Q system (USA).

A G9800A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer was applied to collect fluorescence spectra (Agilent Technologies, USA). UV–vis spectra measurements were carried on a TU-1901 spectrophotometer (PERSEE, China). The Cu-NCDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 F30, USA), X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D8-Advance, Bruker, Germany), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. U.S.A.) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (TENSOR27, Bruker, Germany) to study the morphology, structure and functional groups. Other instruments including centrifuge (80–2, Shanghai surgical instrument factory, China), vortex mixer (XH-B, Hanuo instrument Co., Ltd, China), and pH-meter Sartorius (PB10, Shanghai Precision Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd, China) were used in the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cu, N-doped carbon dots (Cu-NCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu-NCDs was prepared by carrying out a microwave strategy [23]. In short, 0.5 g Cu(CH3COO)2 and different amount of OPD were dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol (A preliminary experiment indicated that the fluorescence intensity of Cu-NCDs is the strongest when the amount of OPD is 0.5 g). After that, the obtained solution was transferred into the Teflon microwave vessel. The temperature was risen to 300 °C in 5 min using 2000 W microwave irradiations and then kept for another 10 min. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 12 min followed by purification with a 0.22 μm membrane. The Cu-NCDs was stored at 4℃ for further applications.

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared by a hydrothermal method [24,25]. FeCl3·6H2O (1.62 g) and NaAc·3H2O (2.4 g) were dissolved in 50 mL of EG. Then, the obtained solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL), which was kept in an oven with 200℃ for 8 h. The reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The products were collected and washed several times with absolute ethanol/water. Finally, Fe3O4 NPs was dried at 60℃ for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tobacco sample purification by magnetic solid phase extraction
ce:section.ce:para: About 0.01 g of shredded tobacco were accurately weighed and added to a 50 mL centrifuge tube. And 20 mL of deionized water was added and placed for10 min. After that, the mixture was in an ultrasonic water bath for 30 min. The supernatant was collected after centrifugation. The obtained solution, named as “nicotiana extracts”, was diluted to 25 mL with distilled water for further purification and analysis.

Subsequently, 100 mg of Fe3O4 suspension was mixed with the prepared sample solution and vortex-mixing for 3 min. Then the Fe3O4 NPs containing coexisting substances were collected by a strong Nd–Fe–B magnet. The supernatant was used for the spectrofluorimetric detection.

50 μL original solution Cu-NCDs was diluted to 5 mL as standard working solution. 20 µL of Cu-NCDs working solution was added to a different concentration of nicotiana solution (0.1–10 μg mL−1), and the solution was made up to 4 mL with pH 8.0 phosphate buffer. After that, this mixture was vortexed thoroughly, followed by incubation for 10 min. Finally, fluorescence spectra were recorded under an excitation of 270 nm and emission wavelengths of 294 nm 580 nm, respectively.

Aqueous quinine sulfate was used as the reference material to measure the quantum yield of Cu-NCDs. The QY of the Quinine sulfate is 0.54 in 0.05 M H2SO4 solution. The calculation formula of QY is as follows [26]:
Q =
Q R
I S
I R
A R
A S
η S 2
η R 2
where subscripts ‘R,’ ‘S,’ ‘η,’ ‘I”, and ‘A’ refer to quinine sulfate, Cu-NCDs, refractive index (1.33 for water), integrated emission intensity, and absorbance, respectively.

The morphology and structure information of the prepared Cu-NCDs was analyzed by the TEM and XRD. Fig. 2 a shows the TEM image of Cu-NCDs. The results showed that the diameters of Cu-NCDs were mainly distributed in the range of 2.5–3.5 nm with an average size of about 3 nm. Fig. S1a shows the XRD pattern of synthesized Cu-NCDs. The XRD pattern clearly indicated that Cu-NCDs displayed an obvious diffraction peak at 2θ = 29.2°, corresponding to the graphite structure [27].

The surface functional groups and the elemental composition of Cu-NCDs were investigated using FTIR and XPS (Fig. 2b and c). As can be seen from Fig. 2b, the broad peaks at 3455 cm−1 and 2934 cm−1 revealed the existence of O–H and N–H groups [28]. Two peaks at 1643 cm−1 and 1434 cm−1 corresponded to the vibrations of CO and CO/CN groups, respectively [29]. As depicted in Fig. 2c, four main peaks at 284 eV, 400 eV, 532 eV, and 933 eV in the spectrum of Cu-NCDs were assigned to the characteristic signals of C1s, O1s, N1s and Cu2p, respectively [23], implying that Cu was on the surface of the Cu-NCDs. Both FTIR spectra and XPS results indicated that there were chemical bonds and functional groups on the surfaces of the as-prepared Cu-NCDs, such as CO, CN, OH and NH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectrum properties and stability of Cu-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the spectrum properties of Cu-NCDs, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum were investigated as shown in Fig. 2d. A characteristic absorption peak was located at 269 nm and typically assigned to the π − π* transitions of CC and C-N bonds of polyaromatic chromophores [27,30]. Two strong emissions at 294 nm and 580 nm could be observed under excitation at 270 nm (Fig. 2d). Furthermore, the quantum yield of the Cu-NCDs was measured to be 30% using quinine sulfate in 0.05 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54) as a reference.

The stability of the Cu-NCDs under different conditions such as ionic strength, temperature and continuous UV exposure has also been researched. The fluorescence intensity of Cu-NCDs with the different concentration of NaCl (0–1.0 M) was recorded, and it is observed that there was no significant change (Fig. S1b). As shown in Fig. S1c, when the Cu-NCDs was exposed at 360 nm for 60 min, the changes of fluorescence intensity were negligible, indicating that the Cu-NCDs has high photostability. Furthermore, no remarkable emission intensity changes were observed at different temperatures (25–65 °C) (Fig. S1d). These results implied the excellent fluorescence stability of Cu-NCDs.

The Cu-NCDs showed two fluorescence emission peaks at 294 nm and 580 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 270 nm, and the fluorescence intensity at 294 nm and 580 nm could be simultaneously quenched by nicotine. As shown in Fig. S2a, fluorescence intensity decreased in pH from 4.0 to 6.0 in the established system, and an increase in pH from 7.0 to 8.0, whereas a gradual decrease in pH from 9.0 to 10.0. The fluorescence intensity of the Cu-NCDs strongly depends on the pH value of the system. This is likely due to the reversible protonation of surface functionalized groups in acid and alkaline medium, which is related to the fluorescence intensity of Cu-NCDs. In addition, nicotine can maintain a molecular state in a neutral environment, which can combine with Cu-NCD to cause fluorescence quenching [6]. Thus, the pH = 8.0 and 10 min of incubation time were chosen for the subsequent studies. Upon the addition of nicotine to Cu-NCDs solution, the fluorescence reduced within 30 min (Fig. S2b). Therefore, 30 min of incubation was sufficient for the quenching test.

Under the optimized condition, different concentrations of nicotine ranging from 0.1 to 10 μg mL−1 were investigated for the quenching of Cu-NCD. The experimental data was used to calculate quenching constant using the Stern − Volmer equation: F/F0 = -0.083X + 0.985 (R2 = 0.994, at 294 nm) and F/F0 = -0.083X + 0.992 (R2 = 0.990, at 580 nm), where F0, F and X are the fluorescence intensity of Cu-NCDs, the fluorescence intensity upon addition of nicotine and the concentration of the fluorescence nicotine (μg mL−1), respectively. Compared with the fluorescence intensity at 580 nm, the determination coefficient at 294 nm was higher and selected for the determination of nicotine. The limit of detection (LOD) was measured to be 0.01 μg mL−1 (3σ/k, in which σ is the standard deviation of the control, and k is the slope of ΔF/F0 against the concentration of nicotine) (Fig. 3 ) [31].

The possible mechanism for Cu-NCDs quenching by nicotine was probably due to inner filter effect (IFE). FTIR and XPS showed that the existence of OH, COOH, NH2 and Cu on the surface of the as-prepared Cu-NCDs, while there are pyridine ring and hydrogenated pyrrole ring in nicotine molecules (Fig. 1). Hydrogen bonds and metal complexes would be formed, resulting high selectivity and sensitivity for the determination of nicotine. Moreover, Fig. 2d shows the large overlaps between the UV–vis spectrum of nicotine (269 nm) and the excitation and emission spectra of the Cu-NCDs (270 nm and 294 nm). The results suggested that the fluorescence quenching could be attributed to the inner filter effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Purification and selectivity of the method
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the interference of the matrix, the purification of nicotiana extract is necessary and can improve the accuracy of method. The purification effect of MSPE technology on tobacco samples was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the absorption intensity of the nicotiana extract was higher than that of purified samples, which could be attributed to the purification of Fe3O4 NPs. Similar to the observations of our previous reports, the magnetic materials could effectively purify the samples [32,33].

The selectivity of the Cu-NCDs toward nicotine was screened by some molecules probably existing in real tobacco samples, including Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Al3+ (Fig. 4b); glucose, maltose, sucrose, glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), methionine (Met) and glutamic (Glu) (Fig. 4c); oxalate, citric acid, succinic acid, malic acid, gallic acid, catechol, phloroglucinol and phenol (Fig. 4d). As can been seen, when the interfering substances’ concentration was 10-fold of nicotine (2 μg mL−1), almost no significant fluorescence changes at 294 nm was observed. These results indicated that the Cu-NCDs has excellent detection selectivity towards nicotine and can serve as a promising fluorescence probe for nicotine detection.

In order to test the feasibility of the proposed method, the Cu-NCDs fluorescence probe for nicotine detection was investigated in real tobacco samples. The recoveries were determined by spiking a series of different concentrations of nicotine standards (0.5, 1, and 4 mg g−1) in real tobacco samples. The detection results are offered in Table 1 . The recoveries of nicotine based on MSPE purification were in the range from 96.46% to 101.9% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) no more than 4.13%. However, the recoveries of the method without MSPE purification ranged from 96.03% to 106.76% with RSD less than 6.12% (n = 5). Compared with the results of without MSPE purification, the excellent recovery and RSD indicated that Cu-NCDs fluorescent probe combined with MSPE could well improve the practicability and repeatability of the prepared method. These results showed that the proposed method is a promising nanoprobe for the detection of nicotine in real tobacco samples with excellent sensitivity and repeatability.

A novel dual-emission Cu-NCDs was synthesized by a facile microwave method, which displayed two fluorescent emission peaks at 294 nm and 580 nm. The quantum yield reached up to 30%. The Cu-NCDs was used as a fluorescence probe for nicotine detection in tobacco samples. The Cu-NCDs selectively responded to nicotine and the mechanism was also investigated. The results showed that the Cu-NCDs remains extremely stable under different conditions and can be used as an anti-interference fluorescent probe for real sample analysis. Furthermore, the Fe3O4 NPs as magnetic adsorbent was utilized to reduce or eliminate the interfering substances in the tobaccos. Compared with the results of without MSPE purification, the method showed better recovery andrepeatability. These results demonstrated the potential of the established method for the detection of nicotine in tobacco samples, but the detection of nicotine in other samples needs further development.

Xueqin Xu: Writing - original draft, Methodology. Zhiyan Chen: Writing - original draft, Methodology. Qiulan Li: Investigation, Validation. Dongling Meng: Formal analysis, Software, Methodology. Honglin Jiang: Formal analysis, Software, Methodology. Yun Zhou: Formal analysis, Software, Methodology. Shouai Feng: Formal analysis, Software, Methodology. Yaling Yang: Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by the project “Research and Application of Fluorescent Probes Based on Carbon Dots” (No. 2019450000370017), and Science and technology project of Guangxi Province (No. GuiKeAB18229002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105708.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
